source_paper_title: A Fast Temporal Decomposition Procedure for Long-Horizon Nonlinear Dynamic Programming
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Na_2024, title={A Fast Temporal Decomposition Procedure for Long-Horizon Nonlinear Dynamic Programming}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1378}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1378}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Na, Sen and Anitescu, Mihai and Kolar, Mladen}, year={2024}, month=may, pages={1012–1044} }
context:
Page 3, Introduction (explicit questions Q1 and Q2)

First, only local convergence guarantee is established while global convergence is still unknown.
In fact, as we will explain later, the Schwarz scheme does not converge globally in general. There is no guarantee that the composed solution of the subproblems is the solution to the full problem, even if we correctly specify the boundary variables for each subproblem. Thus, we arise the question:
Q1: How to design an OTD-based procedure that converges globally?
title: Design OTD-based globally convergent algorithm for long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs
background_latex:
Consider the nonlinear equality-constrained dynamic program over horizon length \(N\):
\[
\min_{x_0,\dots,x_N,\,u_0,\dots,u_{N-1}} \; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} g_k(x_k,u_k) + g_N(x_N)
\]
subject to the dynamics
\[
 x_{k+1}=f_k(x_k,u_k) \quad (k=0,1,\dots,N-1), \qquad x_0=\bar x_0,
\]
where \(x_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_x}\) are states, \(u_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_u}\) are controls, and \(g_k, f_k\) are given (possibly nonlinear, nonconvex) functions.

An overlapping temporal decomposition (OTD) partitions the stage set \([0,N]\) into exclusive subintervals \([n_i,n_{i+1}]\) with knots \(0=n_0<n_1<\cdots<n_M=N\), and extends each exclusive interval by \(b\ge 1\) stages at both ends to form an extended interval \([m_1^i,m_2^i]\) with
\[
 m_1^i=\max\{n_i-b,0\},\qquad m_2^i=\min\{n_{i+1}+b,N\}.
\]
Associated to each extended interval one defines a subproblem parameterized by boundary (interface) variables \(d_i\) that encode values at or near \(m_1^i\) and \(m_2^i\) (e.g., initial state and terminal state/control/dual information). Solving subproblems in parallel and then composing only the exclusive parts \([n_i,n_{i+1})\) yields a full-horizon candidate trajectory.

A key issue is that even if each subproblem is solved to optimality for given boundary variables, the composed solution need not satisfy the Karush--Kuhn--Tucker (KKT) conditions of the full problem unless compatibility conditions hold at knots (agreement of overlapping solutions and appropriate consistency of multipliers).
problem_definition_latex:
Construct an algorithm based on overlapping temporal decomposition of the horizon into extended intervals with overlap size \(b\), which generates iterates \((x^{\tau}_{0:N},u^{\tau}_{0:N-1},\lambda^{\tau}_{0:N})\) for the full-horizon problem and is globally convergent in the sense that, from an arbitrary (not necessarily locally accurate) initialization, the algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a KKT point of the full nonlinear dynamic program (under explicitly stated, verifiable assumptions).

In particular, the goal is to specify:
1) how the subproblems are parameterized and solved across iterations,
2) how subproblem solutions are composed into a full-horizon iterate,
3) what globalization mechanism (e.g., merit function/line search/trust region/coordinator) is used,
so that global convergence to a KKT point is guaranteed for general long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs of the above form.

Consider the nonlinear equality-constrained dynamic program over horizon length \(N\):
\[
\min_{x_0,\dots,x_N,\,u_0,\dots,u_{N-1}} \; \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} g_k(x_k,u_k) + g_N(x_N)
\]
subject to the dynamics
\[
 x_{k+1}=f_k(x_k,u_k) \quad (k=0,1,\dots,N-1), \qquad x_0=\bar x_0,
\]
where \(x_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_x}\) are states, \(u_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_u}\) are controls, and \(g_k, f_k\) are given (possibly nonlinear, nonconvex) functions.

An overlapping temporal decomposition (OTD) partitions the stage set \([0,N]\) into exclusive subintervals \([n_i,n_{i+1}]\) with knots \(0=n_0<n_1<\cdots<n_M=N\), and extends each exclusive interval by \(b\ge 1\) stages at both ends to form an extended interval \([m_1^i,m_2^i]\) with
\[

\]
Associated to each extended interval one defines a subproblem parameterized by boundary (interface) variables \(d_i\) that encode values at or near \(m_1^i\) and \(m_2^i\) (e.g., initial state and terminal state/control/dual information). Construct an algorithm based on overlapping temporal decomposition of the horizon into extended intervals with overlap size \(b\), which generates iterates \((x^{\tau}_{0:N},u^{\tau}_{0:N-1},\lambda^{\tau}_{0:N})\) for the full-horizon problem and is globally convergent in the sense that, from an arbitrary (not necessarily locally accurate) initialization, the algorithm is guaranteed to converge to a KKT point of the full nonlinear dynamic program (under explicitly stated, verifiable assumptions).

In particular, the goal is to specify:
1) how the subproblems are parameterized and solved across iterations,
2) how subproblem solutions are composed into a full-horizon iterate,
3) what globalization mechanism (e.g., merit function/line search/trust region/coordinator) is used,
so that global convergence to a KKT point is guaranteed for general long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs of the above form.
location_in_paper: Page 3, Introduction (explicit questions Q1 and Q2)
open_problem_quote:
First, only local convergence guarantee is established while global convergence is still unknown.
In fact, as we will explain later, the Schwarz scheme does not converge globally in general. There is no guarantee that the composed solution of the subproblems is the solution to the full problem, even if we correctly specify the boundary variables for each subproblem. Thus, we arise the question:
Q1: How to design an OTD-based procedure that converges globally?
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for a constructive globally convergent algorithm rather than the validity of a specific universal inequality. There is no clear finite-dimensional instance space whose search would decisively refute the existence of such a procedure in general; negative results would depend on formal impossibility frameworks not specified in the statement.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms (e.g., interface-update rules, merit functions, line-search/trust-region coordinators) can be represented programmatically and tested on families of benchmark NLDPs with computable stationarity/feasibility residuals as a fitness signal. However, empirical performance is only a proxy for a global convergence guarantee, so automated search can suggest designs but cannot certify the required theorem-level property.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem posed in the introduction of Na–Anitescu–Kolar asks for an overlapping temporal decomposition (OTD) method that is globally convergent for long-horizon nonlinear equality-constrained dynamic programs, i.e., converges from arbitrary initialization to a KKT point while coordinating overlapping subproblem solves so that knot compatibility is enforced in the limit. This is solved by the paper “A Fast Temporal Decomposition Procedure for Long-horizon Nonlinear Dynamic Programming”, which introduces FOTD: an SQP framework using the differentiable exact augmented Lagrangian \(L_\eta(z,\lambda)\) as a merit function and Armijo line search, while approximating each SQP Newton system via OTD on linear-quadratic subproblems over extended intervals with overlap \(b\). The key technical step is proving that the OTD-approximated Newton direction remains a descent direction for \(L_\eta\) when \(b\) and penalty parameters are chosen so the relative direction error \(\delta\) is small (with \(\delta\sim C\rho^b\)), yielding global convergence of the KKT residual \(\|\nabla L(z^\tau,\lambda^\tau)\|\to 0\).

This development builds on earlier OTD/Schwarz work that established exponential decay of sensitivity and local linear contraction but lacked a globalization mechanism. In particular, “On the convergence of overlapping Schwarz decomposition for nonlinear optimal control” proves uniform local linear convergence of a nonlinear overlapping Schwarz iteration (solving nonlinear subproblems to optimality) under sensitivity-decay assumptions, while also highlighting that naive composition can fail globally because KKT compatibility across knots need not hold. The sensitivity foundation is provided by “Exponential decay in the sensitivity analysis of nonlinear dynamic programming”, which shows that primal-dual perturbation effects decay like \(C\rho^{\text{distance}}\) under reduced-Hessian lower bounds, controllability, and boundedness; FOTD leverages this to bound the OTD truncation error in the Newton direction by \(C\rho^b\).

Related strands include truncation/online methods such as “Superconvergence of online optimization for model predictive control”, which uses similar decay-of-sensitivity structure to prove exponential interior accuracy for one-Newton-step receding-horizon schemes, albeit only locally and without OTD composition. Linear-quadratic precursors such as “Exponentially accurate temporal decomposition for long-horizon linear-quadratic dynamic optimization” justify overlap-driven exponential accuracy in convex LQ settings and align with the fact that FOTD’s decomposed subproblems are linear-quadratic Newton systems. Application-driven Schwarz decompositions (e.g., minimum lap-time problems) and general SQP globalization analyses (e.g., stochastic SQP with augmented-Lagrangian merit functions) provide complementary evidence and tools, but the general globally convergent OTD-based algorithmic template for nonlinear dynamic programs is now provided by FOTD.

sources.title: Optimization and Control for Scalable Modeling and Inference in Dynamical Systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Chapter 5 develops a continuous-time overlapping Schwarz decomposition (OSD) method for linear-quadratic optimal control with time-inhomogeneous linear dynamics, formulated via Pontryagin’s Minimum Principle. The main theoretical claim (as summarized in the abstract/intro) is a convergence guarantee for the Schwarz iteration based on an exponential decay of sensitivity (EDS) property of the associated Hamiltonian system, yielding explicit convergence constants and showing faster convergence with larger overlap size.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for an OTD-based globally convergent algorithm for general nonlinear, nonconvex, equality-constrained discrete-time dynamic programs, including a globalization mechanism guaranteeing convergence to a KKT point from arbitrary initialization.

This dissertation does not provide such a general algorithm. Its closest connection is Chapter 5, which studies an overlapping Schwarz/temporal decomposition scheme (continuous-time, optimize-then-discretize) and proves convergence for a restricted class: linear-quadratic control problems (LQ) with time-varying linear dynamics. This is analogous in spirit to OTD/FOTD methods and supports the idea that overlap plus EDS can yield contraction/convergence, but it does not address nonlinear dynamics/costs, discrete-time KKT systems, or global convergence mechanisms (merit/line search/trust region) for nonconvex problems.

sources.title: Schwarz decomposition for parallel minimum lap-time problems: evaluating against ADMM
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only: the paper proposes and evaluates a Schwarz (overlapping domain/time) decomposition method for solving a parallelized Minimum Lap-Time optimal control problem, and compares its performance against ADMM. No theorem-level global convergence guarantee to a KKT point for general nonlinear dynamic programs is stated in the available abstract.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an OTD-based algorithm with a globalization mechanism and a proof of global convergence to a KKT point for general long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs. A Schwarz decomposition is conceptually close to overlapping temporal decomposition (both use overlapping subdomains/subintervals and interface consistency). If the paper formulates MLTP as a long-horizon nonlinear OCP and uses overlapping subproblems with interface variables, it may provide a concrete instantiation of OTD/Schwarz ideas and empirical evidence versus ADMM. However, based on the title/abstract alone, there is no indication of a globally convergent scheme (merit/line-search/trust-region) or a general convergence proof; it appears primarily algorithmic/experimental for a specific application class (MLTP).

sources.title: Asymptotic convergence rate and statistical inference for stochastic sequential quadratic programming
sources.theorem_or_result: For stochastic equality-constrained nonlinear programs \(\min_x \mathbb{E}[f(x;\xi)]\) s.t. \(c(x)=0\), the paper analyzes an adaptive inexact stochastic SQP (AI-StoSQP) method that uses single-sample stochastic gradients/Hessians, randomized sketch-and-project linear solvers for the SQP KKT system, and a random stepsize \(\bar\alpha_t\in[\beta_t,\beta_t+\chi_t]\). Under standard smoothness/constraint qualification/bounded-moment assumptions and stepsize conditions, it proves: (i) almost sure convergence of the KKT residual \(\|\nabla L(x_t,\lambda_t)\|\to 0\) (Theorem 3.7); (ii) local last-iterate rate \(\|(x_t-x^*,\lambda_t-\lambda^*)\|=O(\sqrt{\beta_t\log(1/\beta_t)})+O(\chi_t/\beta_t)\) assuming convergence to a strict local solution (Theorem 4.8/4.14); and (iii) asymptotic normality \(\beta_t^{-1/2}(x_t-x^*,\lambda_t-\lambda_t^*)\Rightarrow \mathcal N(0,\Xi^*)\) with a Berry–Esseen bound and a computable covariance estimator (Theorems 4.11, Lemma 4.12).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an OTD-based (overlapping temporal decomposition) algorithm for long-horizon nonlinear dynamic programs with a globalization mechanism guaranteeing convergence to a KKT point from arbitrary initialization. This paper does not address temporal decomposition, dynamic-program structure, or parallel subproblem composition/compatibility at overlaps. Instead, it provides globalization and convergence theory for a (stochastic) SQP framework for general equality-constrained nonlinear programs, using an augmented-Lagrangian merit function to prove almost sure KKT-residual convergence and giving local rate/CLT results. These results could serve as theoretical tools if one were to build an OTD coordinator that uses SQP/merit-function globalization on the full KKT system or on interface variables, but no such OTD algorithm is designed or analyzed here.
